speaker: interviewee
text: What I supposed to do it And this same thing, I was coming always to the same place. Later, they change the place. And the same thing, exchange, they knew me already. The first thing to obtain weapons, but we didn't have-- the only weapons we had, the gun which I received. And what we was too far for the Allied forces, or for any forces, to make drops. Like in other part of the country, the underground used to get by the plane, armament. No, we couldn't, because it was too far east.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to obtain ourself. But we knew that many farmers had rifles, and guns, and ammunition, and mines even-- no mines-- yeah, one fellow had mines. It was very dangerous, but diffuse mines, he said. And the grenades, which we wanted the German grenades was with the handle. Those were the best grenades.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we supposed to get from the farmers. And they hidden, because war was going on before between Russian and Germans twice, back and forth, and the first Pole with Russians, later Russians with German, later German with Russians, back and forth. So whatever farmers could find which was made out of iron, they hide it, because they could make all different things from the iron-- hooks, or plows. And the people knew among themselves that they-- among themselves, they knew that they be proud of that they hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first of all, I inducted two fellows. This is what I supposed to do it. Who are my assistants? And who are regular army members? They are young people. They didn't have any army experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I knew that their father was big anti-Semite. And they were proud of it. And we call it in Poland [POLISH] they belong to the organization. And he was one of the big [INAUDIBLE] of the organizations. And they hate Jews like their father Jews was hiding. So I knew what company I am in. And so I inducted them. This way, I was inducted. And one of them was my radio operator. And the other was a tough guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first excursion was we went to a farmer, but not which knew, very, very far away during the night. And I knock on the door. And they ask, who is there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, in Polish, German [INAUDIBLE]. And he got scared, and opened the door I jump in with the two fellows in, grab him. He said, don't say a word. We are not German police. We are your friends. We are friends. We understand that you had this, and this, and this. You help yourself, because we came here to protect you. We need it. No, I don't have anything. And he swears that. And I said, your swearing don't help. We know that you have hiding. You tell us where you hide it. And we take it-- we will take it. We will go away. And we'll never see you again. And this was in a different village, which he didn't know us. But before we went, we put with this black suds, our faces were all blackened now. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: He couldn't recognize you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Black people come and-- and we are also in the makeup-- it wasn't a uniform, but like a uniform. Part of a Russian uniform, part of a German uniform. Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: AK, this Armia Krajowa. This is national army. But there were two Polish underground armies. One is national, which is the Armia Krajowa, which didn't accept any Jews, and fought with Jews. Jews were there enemies in this area. And Armia Ludowa, which is the folk's army, which was under communistic regime. And many of-- many of the soldiers who escaped who didn't want to be-- get in the German hands, to be PW, organize the underground. And this was the Polish underground. But the Nazis-- but the Armia Krajowa hate the other armies. They were enemies, because they call them communists. And my original thought was, how to-- how could I join-- how what I can see that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: But by-- not by choice, but I joined the other army. But when I was-- when we went in the woods, and I always said when we had-- when we run into the other units, I said, we will not waste a bullet. We will go the other direction. Our goal is to kill Germans. We don't have too many bullets. This was my excuse to shoot the jerks, or communists. Our main goal is now to fight Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you serve in the Armia Krajowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since '41 till '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your main occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your main activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I'm telling you. First, we had-- first order, we had to had guns. And so I'm telling you the details. Maybe you-- what-- those are how we get the guns. And this [INAUDIBLE] that he doesn't have. At that time, I said, I don't have any other choice. I took out the gun, gave to the assistant. And I said, when I said-- said, two, three, you have to execute you. But I-- but before I said, say your last prayer. Naturally, he was crying, and praying. But this was all makeup. Sorry, it is the only way. But when I said-- when he finished the prayer, I said one, oh, he came running.

speaker: interviewer
text: It worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: And always, but this what we're hiding, always in such conditions. And we couldn't use it. We had to clean it, and repair it. And, naturally, the boys didn't know about how to do it. But I had experience with guns. And oil was very hard to get, the proper oil, and paper to clean it, and other things. But somehow, we made to-- we didn't know how it would work, because we could never try it. We could never make any noise. But the pin was working, everything. And, finally, we did this with many farmers, and always the same story. He beg, he kneel, he pray that he doesn't have anything to believe him. But until I came I said say your last prayer, this was that he knew that it's no joke. And when I was giving him the gun, I said, I would say, two, three, naturally he order the gun never to shoot. This was the order. But [INAUDIBLE] we would never shoot person. First of all, we wouldn't have wanted to make a noise. The worst enemy of the noise were the dogs. This would have been afraid. And I remember I had to shoot one dog. And I still remember. I came-- he was on the tied up, and barking. So I was afraid he wake all village. And I came close, closer I came, the more close I aim, the more he bark. And finally, I aim it with one. I knew I could only with one shot. One shot, and drop. And this picture in my eye--

speaker: interviewer
text: Reminds?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this was-- one moment. He was innocent dog protecting his property, his master. And here--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to kill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to shoot him. But there was no any other way. And I was lucky that I shot him with one shot, because I aim long. And he moved. And it wasn't easy. And we had only one weak light, because batter was hard to get. And we had to save the battery. So we always operate-- and, finally, when we hit our-- our arsenal, how we call it. We had to hide also the arsenal. And hide [INAUDIBLE]. And the next order was-- one other thing. I want to chronologically to-- one day I was outside. And I saw two officers walking in with their guns. This scared me, because they never walk-- the Lithuanian did walk with the guns, night and day. We knew about it. But here, Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to them. And I ask in German if I can help them with something. I said, you are looking for something. I can help them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fellow farmer talks to them in perfect German. And he say, it's good what I'm doing here. He say, I'm working here on the fire. I'm put by the German authorities, naturally, because it was the German [INAUDIBLE]. And I am in charge of the fish hatcheries. And they said they came to hunt for the wild duck. We had many wild ducks over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Russians were not-- German officers. And I spoke to them in German. And so I told them, if they will come next day early morning, I have the dog. We had two dogs. I will raise the dog. We never let the dog loose. But I knew if I would let them loose, the first thing he would be running after the ducks. And then I would be able to shoot. And they ask me more about myself. And I was telling him. And I am here in charge. Therefore, I know about the ducks. And they told me that they are from this unit from the barracks, because not far away they had a depot barracks. This was-- they had all arsenal, and the troops which have convoys which have been going east. They had been, like, a point, stop point, and going the other way. And so I said, no, you cannot go to your base. I invite them inside. In the beginning, they are a little leery. But I assure them that nobody is their enemy here, because we are all here working on-- and I made scrambled eggs, and fresh bread, and butter, and whisky. I tell them, we have only moonshine. We don't have any good whiskey. Next time, they brought whiskey. But-- and they were-- next the morning, they came. It's three of them, next day. But they had four guns. And they gave me-- and this is for you. I said, I am not allowed. I said, when the Lithuanians will come, they will arrest me here. They-- to this respect, the Lithuanian were so suspicious of anything. And so most of times, they was coming drunk. One evening, they came, and went through the whole hut. And here on the wall were-- I don't remember how many-- hats, winter hats, for Sunday hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, how many people are you here? I said three of us. The two boys, and I am sleeping here. No, they argue that more people here. Where are you hiding those people? Who they are? For a long time. And, finally, they said, you see how many hats here? There are more than three of you-- philosophy. So I explained to them, this is for Sunday. This is for winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is for work. And this is his. This is mine. Finally, they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew that the-- so I told them, I'm afraid to keep it, the gun. No, if something will come, tell them to us. So I put two nails, and put right in front over the gun so they wouldn't be-- Surprised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but never happens that they came. And I never hunt, even with the Germans. I said, I don't know how to use a gun. So I'm afraid. But later, they took with them. But in the beginning, I never hunt. I didn't want to show them that I know how to use a gun. And I didn't want it. For some reason, I-- but we become friends. As they used to come quite often hunting, or just for [GERMAN] for-- because they had the German Russians, here was everything fresh. Later, they wanted to [INAUDIBLE] some meat, some [INAUDIBLE] fat to send to Germany. And they used to bring things we could use on the farm. And-- and we start talking about politics. And this-- you ask me before, how do-- how do I knew what happened-

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have been telling me everything, without asking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything what I would-- about the camps. I didn't know nothing about-- oh, I knew little bit. We had an underground newspaper was once in a few months printed a little, very [INAUDIBLE]. And it was-- but it wasn't mainly about the underground, but not what about the camps also. But I didn't know details. But they've been telling me all the details. Like, for example, when it was a Jewish holiday. Naturally, I didn't know what is Jewish holiday, the Yom Kippur. They, oh, it was Yom Kippur. I don't know how they-- they said, but with a German . Accent but they knew the name. And at that time, many Jews were killed, and-- and-- and they knew about the Kristallnacht, which was before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they've been telling me all the details. I didn't know nothing about it, about the Jews. Why you are killing Jews? But they are all communists. They are enemies of the German race. That was their answer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was their reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their answer, their answer. Later, after the war when Germans said they didn't know nothing about it, it's not true. They knew. If they knew, they-- the-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you accept-- did you believe them, what they were telling you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I said, why do you kill Jews as a Pole? But-- so they explain to me, and also how many camps were, where the camps where. I didn't ask them. I listened like I wasn't interested. But once, when it was something big, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember something, really, that impressed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the things they have been telling me, that Jews being killed, Jews being taken to camps. And they didn't kill-- they-- about the ovens, I didn't know about that. They didn't tell me, because they had been labor camps. But if some were not behaving, [INAUDIBLE] they will be executed. And many of them have been unbehaving. So they have been shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was what they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what they told me. And also, I said, and what-- were there was a cemetery? No, no, were ovens. They hygienic. They been burned. They-- like-- so the only what was going on, I knew also. For example, when was ghetto, liquidated ghetto, I didn't know about it, only from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where your parents went, what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't know what happened to family. I found out after the war. I have no contact. We didn't have no exchange. They didn't know where I am. And I didn't know where they are. But I knew that-- therefore, when I came home right after '44, immediately I went home, because I hope that I will--

speaker: interviewer
text: Find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start-- and I was going out for orders. And the first order was to destroy the-- no, not destroy, to interrupt communication line. And the main communication line was a telephone line, which was used only for Air Force. And-- and-- and they knew at that time must be some planned some attacks, or some invasion, or something. And at that time, destroy, interrupt the communication line. There were a post.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you do? You take a saw, and cut the post. And the post fell down. And we did it in a few places, not-- not very far, but-- and what happened, they took-- the first things they took, a few people, I remember, from the village, where this was close, and shot them for sabotage. So we knew we cannot work in the places where some people live. Must be someplace where no villages, where only fields are-- and we like to work always from the woods, because we can-- you come out for the foods. And you run away to hide in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they repair very quickly. They took people to work. And they-- so next time, we got from Vilna such-- you put on your--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoes, It's and you can walk on the post. So when we cut the wire in many places. Naturally, the patrols were coming. So they saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they repaired the-- so this wouldn't work. So we-- later, we hear the order to disrupt communication. We cut the wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had-- but we-- so the wire wouldn't fall, first, we tied up to the post, and to the wire, to the-- and after the wire was attached, we cut in half so it was interruption. But you couldn't see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had instruments, I don't know what kind of instruments, that work like radars, that they know how far. So-- but it was longer to repair. And-- but it was cat and mouse. So the Germans decided they had so much trouble with the wires, they put a cable underground. I remember, because I saw how they put the cables. It was with lead. So we had the order-- we didn't know how to do it. We were thinking, OK, dig the hole, and cut again with wires. But we had the orders to actually [INAUDIBLE] got acid. Said, dig out a hole in a few places, very far apart, also no-- where is a woods, where are no-- so they would know that it's not one village that-- but in different places. And pour not up to the end, but so-- leave little bit soil. And put the acid. And the acid will find its way to the-- so here was eating, after a while, in the other place. They're not--

speaker: interviewer
text: They can't find it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. So this was very undependable. So they abandoned the cable, and came back to the up. Later, we had order to not destroy, but to-- they knew when the convoys are coming. I also-- later I was also helpful, because I have some Germans, we become very-- they called me our friend, my friend. And they think is that I am Volksdeutsche because I told them that my mother was German, which is true. My father was Pole. So they would say in cases like that, was [INAUDIBLE] Volksdeutsche. But I said, no, I am a Pole, because I was Polish citizen. But I knew German, because I told him I had in the school, and I had in Vienna. I didn't hide what it. And so they accepted that I-- why I am speaking so well German. And they knew where I come from, and what my father did. So they call me always-- and they was telling me things which I didn't ask. And we knew where they expecting the convoys. But our underground has also-- they knew also. So slow down the convoys. When we slow down the convoys, the convoys which have been going on to Eastern Front, from west to east, supply or other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 19-- started in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the convoys?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was after '44, all the activities, to June, 1944. And so we had-- we received nails to spread the nails on the road, but not-- so they-- they had to stop, and change the tires, and went on the way. But we slow down. But later, didn't work, because they had the spares. So we had been told to do it in many places, so they would run out of the spares, and had to stop and repair the tires. And-- but this still wasn't so efficient. Oh, but what they did-- later, they came with the magnets on front of the convoys with magnets and brushes, big wire brushes, like a cleaning of the street with the-- with the such instrument. The wires were on the brushes, and after a-- like a comb to put the brushes in the container which was behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this didn't work. So we had a nail which looks like a x, but squash x-- no, z, letter z, squashed, so aluminum. So the-- so the magnet wouldn't grab it. So they came with the brushes, later the brushes. And-- but in meantime, but we did in many, many places. But I had quite a run when I-- once I suppose to pick up the brushes from-- from-- from a Vilna. And this was in some-- in the back of the house, in the backyard. And-- OK.
